title: 請廣傳！一定要長期保持身體溫暖

replies.msg:
大家都知隻病毒會潛伏，但潛伏幾耐，大量複製嘅條件係乜，大家又知唔知？

其實隻病毒 somehow 識 detect 我地嘅 immune system，雖然任何病毒都無生命，但佢地都好「聰明」，研究病毒嘅學者至今仍未知點解會咁 ....

不過知道佢地嘅 behaviour 其實已經都算幾好，就係佢地入左身體後第一時間就係 detect 我地嘅免疫系統，同埋佢地自己友嘅數量 .... 無錯！佢地做緊嘅就係比較緊敵我強弱，我知係難以至信 .... biologist 知道後都 O 曬嘴 .... 不過佢地就係咁「聰明」

呢個分析敵我實力差距嘅階段就係所謂嘅「潛伏期」

咁所以，好喇，收集曬資訊，做曬分析，咁點？
夠贏面，就會大舉入侵細胞，免疫系統 detect 到細胞異常，開始免疫反應，病徵陸逐出現。
否則，繼續無限期潛伏，只要唔引起白血球注意，佢地就唔會被殺「死」（其實係分解，病毒無生命，何來死？）咁樣就知點解蝙蝠可以同體內長期都有多種致命病毒共存，就係因為病毒入左去之後知道打唔過，索性無限期潛伏

點解知道打唔過？因為蝙蝠嘅免疫系統係被研究人員稱為超級免疫系統，其中一個原因係佢地身體長期發燒！作為唯一一種識飛嘅 mammal, 佢地飛行時身體會發熱致 40 deg C，令佢地嘅免疫系統長期活躍

所以我地嘅身體都一樣，但人類正常體溫去唔到 40 度，但長期維持身體溫暖加埋健康嘅飲食同充足嘅睡眠，其實已經好足夠令體內嘅病毒（如有）保持不活躍狀態

所以請大家每日都要沖熱水涼至少 15 分鐘，多飲水多食生果，多做運動，著夠衫，帶口罩，避免去人多嘅地方，每日 keep 住呢啲規則，捱過冬天就會無事！

[合法罷工 ， 助己助人] [送你的命給狗官] [染病的你、升官的他]
[少部份醫護已開始罷工、請珍惜機會、接力更多人響應]
本身你在醫院/海關/巴士司機/ 金融/酒店/運輸/任何政府崗位工作
又有傷風感冒症狀 （很容易自製冷親)
請上齊裝備即刻去急症室睇醫生 醫生一定畀你嚇親畀假紙你 甚至將你隔離 變相罷工， 即時救港行動
只要你有輕微發燒症狀， 拍片做證 ，你老細就吹你唔脹， 你問:係唔係要返工傳染畀同事先至安樂？
病假救港、助己助人、刻不容緩

我所知既係蝙蝠係可以靠自身飛行既哺乳類動物。
所以需要極大量運動,而呢種高能量運動會破壞生物既DNA

於是為左避免免疫系統長期都打緊仗,因此進化成一種較暖和既免疫系統去停止排除呢類DNA(簡單講就係降低保護等級),最終導致蝙蝠可以與多種病毒共存。(而唔係長期發燒所以免疫系統長期活躍)

quote.msg:
大家都知隻病毒會潛伏，但潛伏幾耐，大量複製嘅條件係乜，大家又知唔知？

其實隻病毒 somehow 識 detect 我地嘅 immune system，雖然任何病毒都無生命，但佢地都好「聰明」，研究病毒嘅學者至今仍未知點解會咁 ....

不過知道佢地嘅 behaviour 其實已經都算幾好，就係佢地入左身體後第一時間就係 detect 我地嘅免疫系統，同埋佢地自己友嘅數量 .... 無錯！佢地做緊嘅就係比較緊敵我強弱，我知係難以至信 .... biologist 知道後都 O 曬嘴 .... 不過佢地就係咁「聰明」

呢個分析敵我實力差距嘅階段就係所謂嘅「潛伏期」

咁所以，好喇，收集曬資訊，做曬分析，咁點？
夠贏面，就會大舉入侵細胞，免疫系統 detect 到細胞異常，開始免疫反應，病徵陸逐出現。
否則，繼續無限期潛伏，只要唔引起白血球注意，佢地就唔會被殺「死」（其實係分解，病毒無生命，何來死？）咁樣就知點解蝙蝠可以同體內長期都有多種致命病毒共存，就係因為病毒入左去之後知道打唔過，索性無限期潛伏

點解知道打唔過？因為蝙蝠嘅免疫系統係被研究人員稱為超級免疫系統，其中一個原因係佢地身體長期發燒！作為唯一一種識飛嘅 mammal, 佢地飛行時身體會發熱致 40 deg C，令佢地嘅免疫系統長期活躍

所以我地嘅身體都一樣，但人類正常體溫去唔到 40 度，但長期維持身體溫暖加埋健康嘅飲食同充足嘅睡眠，其實已經好足夠令體內嘅病毒（如有）保持不活躍狀態

所以請大家每日都要沖熱水涼至少 15 分鐘，多飲水多食生果，多做運動，著夠衫，帶口罩，避免去人多嘅地方，每日 keep 住呢啲規則，捱過冬天就會無事！

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6410205/

2. Bats Have an Efficient and Varied Antiviral Response
It has been observed that viruses which severely affect other mammals, including humans, are apparently nonpathogenic for bats [20]. This adaptability of bats to harbor many viruses without showing overt pathology suggests that bats have evolved immune mechanisms that allow for benign virus–host relationships.

The immune response has two primary components, innate and adaptive. The host initially responds to infection by activating innate mechanisms. Genes such as those for sensing and repairing DNA damage and the inflammatory process are under positive selection in black flying foxes and David’s myotis (Myotis davidii) [21]. For instance, there are mutations in the coding sequence of p53 functional domains that are unique to these bats. Interferons are the primary innate effector molecules that control viral replication. Several types of interferon have been identified in bats, especially Type I and Type III. Type I interferon is induced after virus infection in Egyptian fruit bats whereas Type III interferon is induced similarly in pteropid bats [22,23]. It is interesting to note that interferon genes in black flying foxes have contracted in terms of diversity. The number of variants of interferon present in these bats is lower compared to those in the gene loci of ten other vertebrate species. Despite this decrease in interferon gene diversity, black flying foxes express these variants at higher basal levels than other mammalian species. This suggests that the interferon and interferon stimulated genes are constitutively expressed in these bats [18]. In contrast, a recent study of the genome of Egyptian fruit bats shows expanded diversity of Type I interferon genes. This, as well as expansion of various other immune genes, suggests novel modes of antiviral defense in bats of various species [24]. Novel modes of antiviral defenses could exist in other species of bats and future studies would help in revealing them.

2. Bats Have an Efficient and Varied Antiviral Response
It has been observed that viruses which severely affect other mammals, including humans, are apparently nonpathogenic for bats [20]. This adaptability of bats to harbor many viruses without showing overt pathology suggests that bats have evolved immune mechanisms that allow for benign virus–host relationships.

The immune response has two primary components, innate and adaptive. The host initially responds to infection by activating innate mechanisms. Genes such as those for sensing and repairing DNA damage and the inflammatory process are under positive selection in black flying foxes and David’s myotis (Myotis davidii) [21]. For instance, there are mutations in the coding sequence of p53 functional domains that are unique to these bats. Interferons are the primary innate effector molecules that control viral replication. Several types of interferon have been identified in bats, especially Type I and Type III. Type I interferon is induced after virus infection in Egyptian fruit bats whereas Type III interferon is induced similarly in pteropid bats [22,23]. It is interesting to note that interferon genes in black flying foxes have contracted in terms of diversity. The number of variants of interferon present in these bats is lower compared to those in the gene loci of ten other vertebrate species. Despite this decrease in interferon gene diversity, black flying foxes express these variants at higher basal levels than other mammalian species. This suggests that the interferon and interferon stimulated genes are constitutively expressed in these bats [18]. In contrast, a recent study of the genome of Egyptian fruit bats shows expanded diversity of Type I interferon genes. This, as well as expansion of various other immune genes, suggests novel modes of antiviral defense in bats of various species [24]. Novel modes of antiviral defenses could exist in other species of bats and future studies would help in revealing them.

This suggests that the interferon and interferon stimulated genes are constitutively expressed in these bats [18]. In contrast, a recent study of the genome of Egyptian fruit bats shows expanded diversity of Type I interferon genes. This, as well as expansion of various other immune genes, suggests novel modes of antiviral defense in bats of various species [24]. Novel modes of antiviral defenses could exist in other species of bats and future studies would help in revealing them.

replies.msg:
WIKI:
病毒對宿主細胞的結構和生化成分會產生廣泛的影響，[34] 這些影響稱為「細胞病變效應」。[35] 多數病毒感染最終會導致宿主細胞死亡。死亡的方式有裂解、細胞膜病變和凋亡（即「自殺」）等。[36]通常宿主細胞死亡是因為病毒合成的蛋白質終止了它們的正常生命活動，這些蛋白質不一定是組成病毒顆粒的蛋白質。[37]

有些病毒不會使宿主細胞發生明顯變化。這些病毒潛伏在細胞里，活動並不活躍。在該階段，細胞通常功能正常，且基本不會出現感染跡象。[38]病毒會潛伏數月乃至數年，這會引起長期感染，皰疹病毒就往往表現如此。[39][40]

有些病毒，如人類皰疹病毒第四型（EB病毒）常會導致細胞增生，但卻不會造成惡性腫瘤[41]。不過，已證實人類乳頭瘤病毒等病毒確有致癌作用。[42] 當病毒將宿主細胞的DNA破壞到該細胞無法自我修復時，這個細胞就會凋亡。細胞發生凋亡時，它會徹底破壞受損的DNA。有些病毒擁有限制細胞凋亡的機制，使宿主細胞在完成子代病毒的合成之前不會死亡。HIV（愛滋病病毒）就有這樣的機制。[36]

推,
同埋唔好比喉嚨有乾痛既機會,
一乾一痛即食龍角散涼下個喉嚨,
體質寒底既可以keep喉嚨成日都涼朕朕,
M記就最好食少D了, 2星期先1次.

我所知既係蝙蝠係可以靠自身飛行既哺乳類動物。
所以需要極大量運動,而呢種高能量運動會破壞生物既DNA
而生物既免疫系統係會自發性排除壞左既DNA
於是為左避免免疫系統長期都打緊仗,因此進化成一種較暖和既免疫系統去停止排除呢類DNA(簡單講就係降低保護等級),最終導致蝙蝠可以與多種病毒共存。(而唔係長期發燒所以免疫系統長期活躍)

shit, 你啱 ....

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6410205/

2. Bats Have an Efficient and Varied Antiviral Response
It has been observed that viruses which severely affect other mammals, including humans, are apparently nonpathogenic for bats [20]. This adaptability of bats to harbor many viruses without showing overt pathology suggests that bats have evolved immune mechanisms that allow for benign virus–host relationships.

The immune response has two primary components, innate and adaptive. The host initially responds to infection by activating innate mechanisms. Genes such as those for sensing and repairing DNA damage and the inflammatory process are under positive selection in black flying foxes and David’s myotis (Myotis davidii) [21]. For instance, there are mutations in the coding sequence of p53 functional domains that are unique to these bats. Interferons are the primary innate effector molecules that control viral replication. Several types of interferon have been identified in bats, especially Type I and Type III. Type I interferon is induced after virus infection in Egyptian fruit bats whereas Type III interferon is induced similarly in pteropid bats [22,23]. It is interesting to note that interferon genes in black flying foxes have contracted in terms of diversity. The number of variants of interferon present in these bats is lower compared to those in the gene loci of ten other vertebrate species. Despite this decrease in interferon gene diversity, black flying foxes express these variants at higher basal levels than other mammalian species. This suggests that the interferon and interferon stimulated genes are constitutively expressed in these bats [18]. In contrast, a recent study of the genome of Egyptian fruit bats shows expanded diversity of Type I interferon genes. This, as well as expansion of various other immune genes, suggests novel modes of antiviral defense in bats of various species [24]. Novel modes of antiviral defenses could exist in other species of bats and future studies would help in revealing them.

2. Bats Have an Efficient and Varied Antiviral Response
It has been observed that viruses which severely affect other mammals, including humans, are apparently nonpathogenic for bats [20]. This adaptability of bats to harbor many viruses without showing overt pathology suggests that bats have evolved immune mechanisms that allow for benign virus–host relationships.

The immune response has two primary components, innate and adaptive. The host initially responds to infection by activating innate mechanisms. Genes such as those for sensing and repairing DNA damage and the inflammatory process are under positive selection in black flying foxes and David’s myotis (Myotis davidii) [21]. For instance, there are mutations in the coding sequence of p53 functional domains that are unique to these bats. Interferons are the primary innate effector molecules that control viral replication. Several types of interferon have been identified in bats, especially Type I and Type III. Type I interferon is induced after virus infection in Egyptian fruit bats whereas Type III interferon is induced similarly in pteropid bats [22,23]. It is interesting to note that interferon genes in black flying foxes have contracted in terms of diversity. The number of variants of interferon present in these bats is lower compared to those in the gene loci of ten other vertebrate species. Despite this decrease in interferon gene diversity, black flying foxes express these variants at higher basal levels than other mammalian species. This suggests that the interferon and interferon stimulated genes are constitutively expressed in these bats [18]. In contrast, a recent study of the genome of Egyptian fruit bats shows expanded diversity of Type I interferon genes. This, as well as expansion of various other immune genes, suggests novel modes of antiviral defense in bats of various species [24]. Novel modes of antiviral defenses could exist in other species of bats and future studies would help in revealing them.
